plainMeaning: People who keep in mind the feet of Pārśva, the teacher of the whole world, have nothing to fear from fire.
recitation: jaga-guruṇo kama-jualaṁ, nivvāvia sayala tihuaṇābhoaṁ | je saṁbharaṁti maṇuā, na kuṇai jalaṇo bhayaṁ tesiṁ ||

wordByWord.gloss: of the world-guru, i.e. of Pārśva, teacher of the whole world
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jagad-guru (jagat 'world' + guru 'teacher'); Prakrit genitive sg. -uṇo for -oḥ
wordByWord.notes: Epithet chosen here for its universal reach — the one whose grace cools all three worlds.

wordByWord.gloss: the pair of feet (object of remembrance)
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. krama-yugala (krama 'step, foot' + yugala 'pair'); = the calaṇa-juala refrain; accusative sg.
wordByWord.notes: Not 'forest-fire': kama = krama 'foot', juala = yugala 'pair'. A popular web paraphrase mis-glosses this as fire; rejected.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. nirvāpita / nirvāpya, from ni-√vā 'to cool, put out'; absolutive qualifying the feet
wordByWord.notes: The pivot word: it sets 'cooling' directly against jalaṇo 'fire' below.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. sakala 'whole'; Prakrit loss of intervocalic -k-

wordByWord.gloss: the (whole) expanse of the three worlds
wordByWord.etymology: Skt. tribhuvana-ābhoga (tihuaṇa = tribhuvana 'three worlds' + ābhoa = ābhoga 'expanse, full extent'); accusative, object of nivvāvia
wordByWord.notes: sayala + tihuaṇābhoaṁ together = 'the entire expanse of the three worlds' that the feet have cooled.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. ye, relative pronoun nom. pl.
Skt. saṁsmaranti, from saṁ-√smṛ 'to recollect'; 3rd pl. present
wordByWord.notes: The act of smaraṇa that the whole Smaraṇa-stotra turns on.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. manujāḥ ('born of Manu'); nom. pl., subject of saṁbharaṁti
Skt. karoti, from √kṛ 'to do'; Prakrit kuṇai, 3rd sg. present
wordByWord.notes: Subject is jalaṇo; 'fire does not make fear for them.'

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. jvalana 'that which burns', from √jval 'to blaze'; nom. sg. -o
wordByWord.notes: The specific peril of this verse-pair — picking up the forest-fire painted in the preceding verse.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. bhaya, from √bhī 'to fear'; accusative sg.
wordByWord.notes: The mahābhaya the whole bhaya-hara stotra dissolves.

wordByWord.etymology: Skt. teṣām, genitive/dative pl. of tad; here 'for them'
wordByWord.notes: Refers back to je maṇuā — the rememberers.

literalTranslation: The world-guru's pair of feet — which have cooled the entire expanse of the three worlds — the people who remember them, fire makes no fear for them.
idiomaticTranslation: The feet of the Lord of all the world, whose grace has cooled the burning expanse of the three realms — for those who hold them in remembrance, fire stirs no fear at all.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā delivers the rescue-turn of the fire-pair whose blaze the preceding verse painted (the wind-whipped forest inferno). The whole verse pivots on a single deliberate contrast: nivvāvia, 'having cooled,' set against jalaṇo, 'fire.' The poet does not say the Jina puts out this particular flame; he says something larger — that the Lord's very feet have already 'cooled the entire expanse of the three worlds' (sayala tihuaṇābhoaṁ). The cooling is cosmic and prior; the rescue from an earthly fire is merely its corollary. Hence the epithet chosen here is jaga-guruṇo, the teacher of the whole world, not a narrower title: it is precisely as universal cooler of the three realms that Pārśva neutralises a local blaze. The means of refuge is, once more, the 'pair of feet' (kama-juala) — the recurring foothold the devotee clings to — and the act is saṁbharaṁti, remembrance. The logic is compact and complete: those who recollect feet that have quenched the burning of all worlds find that fire 'makes no fear' for them. Note the grammatical economy — fire (jalaṇo) is itself the subject that fails to produce fear (na kuṇai bhayaṁ), as if the element itself is disarmed before the rememberer.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The 'cooling' here is doctrinally double. Literally it promises bodily safety from flame; spiritually it evokes the Jina as the vītarāga whose presence quenches the fire of the passions (kaṣāya-agni) that scorches saṁsāra. Because the inner fire of rāga-dveṣa is already stilled in one who truly takes refuge, the outer fire loses its grip — equanimity (praśama) is the real fire-break. The verse thus enacts the stotra's governing principle that smaraṇa of the Jina's feet is itself efficacious, the name-and-feet functioning as a perfected protective formula; merit (puṇya) generated by such devotion is the mechanism by which 'fire makes no fear.'
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Facing literal danger or the inner heat of anger and craving, the reciter fixes attention on the 'cooling feet' of the Jina, letting nivvāvia become the felt antidote to whatever burns. Chanted steadily, the line trains the mind to meet alarm with praśama rather than panic — turning a moment of dread into an act of refuge.

crossReferences.text: v.18 — the summary verse listing fire (jalaṇa) among the perils stilled by reciting Pārśva's name
crossReferences.source: Namiūṇa (Bhayahara) Stotra, Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha Panch Pratikraman ed.

crossReferences.text: v.3 — the burnt-mountain-regreening simile, the stotra's paired image of restoration after fire
Upasargahara Stotra (4th Smaraṇa) — companion Pārśva hymn on refuge in his feet/name
crossReferences.source: Nava Smaraṇa, Anchalgachchha Panch Pratikraman ed.

notes: kamajualaṁ = krama-yugala (Skt. caraṇa-yugala), 'pair of feet' — the same refuge-motif glossed in the preface as calaṇa-juala; a popular AI/web paraphrase wrongly renders it 'forest-fire' and is rejected. nivvāvia is taken as an absolutive/qualifier governing 'sayala tihuaṇābhoaṁ' (the feet 'having cooled the whole expanse of the three worlds'); some renderings instead read the cooling as an attribute of the feet — sense is equivalent. jalaṇo is construed as the grammatical subject of na kuṇai ('fire makes no [fear]'). Spellings (કમજુઅલં, નિવ્વાવિઅ, જલણો, તેસિં) follow the source book verbatim and may differ from other recensions; verse numbering (7) per the supplied text.
